_changelog: v1 — Initial build. First lesson introducing multi-paragraph narrative structure. Introduces all_instances_min_chars validation rule (schema v1.2) in challenge step. Warm-up recalls CSS from Lesson 2. Step 2C anatomy callback in Step 1 info activates prior skeleton knowledge.

meta.title: Write Your Story! 📝
meta._xp_note: Step editors define 80 XP (3 × 20) + challenge 20 XP = 80 XP. total_xp is 100 per PRD spec. Same open question as lessons 001 and 002 — remaining 20 XP source unresolved. Flagged in PRD Section 11. CI XP sum validator will catch this.
meta.objective: By the end — you will write a story with three paragraphs.

prompt_lines: Add a color to your h1.
One CSS rule inside a style tag.

lines: A heading tells WHAT the page is about.
When I learned about paragraphs I wrote one sentence — forty words long.
The browser tried its best. It was not great.

steps.title: One p. One Idea.

lines: Every great page has a story.
This is how you tell it.

steps._animation_note: Three p tags appear one at a time inside a body region. Each fades in with its text already written: first p 'My dog Biscuit loves to run.' Second p 'He is fast and very loud.' Third p 'He once ate my homework. No joke.' After all three are visible, a subtle bracket appears on the right labeled 'a story.' Satisfying, sequential, shows the concept in action.

elements.token: He once ate my homework. No joke.
elements.label: idea 3

steps.prompt: What does each p tag hold?

lines: What does each p tag hold?

correct.byte_line: One idea. Stack a few — you have a story.

incorrect.byte_line: One p tag. One idea. That's the whole rule.

steps.title: h1 and p Work Together
steps._design_note: Piaget — concrete before abstract. Show the relationship visually before any typing. The skeleton callback (using the labeled blueprint from Lesson 1 Step 2C) activates prior knowledge and lowers cognitive load. Ausubel advance organizer theory.
content_lines: h1 says **what** the page is about.
steps._animation_note: Left side: h1 tag appears — 'My Dog Biscuit' — labeled 'THE TOPIC'. A downward arrow. Then a p tag appears below — 'Biscuit loves to run in the park.' — labeled 'THE STORY'. Simple, clear, concrete. No more than 3 seconds per element.

elements.token: Biscuit loves to run in the park.
elements.label: THE STORY

correct.byte_line: p tells the story. h1 names the topic.

incorrect.byte_line: h1 is the title. p is everything after that.

steps.title: Your First Paragraph
content_lines:
Add one `

` tag with one sentence about it.
steps.prompt_code: I love dogs because they are loyal.
steps._starter_note: Starter code has h1 already present with placeholder text. Cursor blinking on line 3. Student does not need to write the h1 — it's given. This reduces cognitive load: they focus on the p tag only. The h1 placeholder 'My Topic' is intentionally generic — students should personalize it but validation does not require it at this step.
steps.hint:
Type `

` then your sentence then `

` to close it.

validation_rules._min_note: Minimum 5 chars for p tags in this lesson — longer than Lessons 1-2 (which used min:2) because we are teaching sentence-level writing. 'Hi.' passes min:2 but is not a sentence about a topic. 5 chars catches single-word entries while allowing very short sentences like 'I like cats.' (12 chars).

correct.byte_line: One paragraph. One idea. Your page is starting to say something.

steps.title: Add Another One
content_lines: Something different — a fact, a question, anything.
steps._starter_note: Starter shows h1 and one p already present — represents the student's work from Step 3. The placeholder text 'Your first paragraph here.' is used because we cannot carry the actual student input forward in the lesson definition (that's a runtime concern). The validation only checks for tag_count p min:2, so the placeholder p satisfies the first p and the student adds the second.
steps.hint:
Add a new line. Type `

` then something new then `

`.

correct.byte_line: Two ideas on the same page. That's called paragraphs. Real ones.

steps._bootstrap_note: 60s median is deliberate — bug-finding takes longer for 7-year-olds than writing. They have to scan the code visually, identify the difference between the two p tags, and recognize the missing closing tag. This is a higher-order task. The 30s hint delay gives them a real attempt before Byte intervenes.
steps.title: Find the Missing Buddy
content_lines: One tag is missing its buddy.
steps.starter_code:
My Favorite Sport

I love basketball.

It is fast and exciting.
steps._bug_note:
The bug: first p tag is missing its closing

. The second p tag is correctly closed. Some browsers will attempt to auto-close the first p when they encounter the second opening p tag — the page may look correct visually. This is intentional: kids learn that correct syntax matters even when the browser is forgiving. The validator checks tag pairing via DOM parsing, not visual output. Implementation: parse the HTML into a DOM, querySelectorAll('p'), check each element has a corresponding closing tag in the source. Alternatively: check that the number of opening p tags equals the number of closing p tags via regex count on the source string as a secondary check — but DOM parsing is preferred.

lines: One tag is missing its buddy.

steps.hint:
Find the p tag without a little line at the end. Add `

` to close it.

validation_rules._note: This rule checks DOM-parsed tag integrity. If the first p is unclosed, querySelectorAll('p') may return unexpected results depending on browser parser behavior. The validator should cross-check: count of '' in source. Flag discrepancy as missing_closing_tag.

correct.byte_line: Found it. Every tag needs its buddy. Every time.

steps._bootstrap_note: 120s median (2 minutes) is the highest in the curriculum so far. Writing three distinct sentences about a chosen topic is a genuine creative writing task for a 7-year-old. The traffic light threshold should use this value — a student spending 4 minutes on the challenge is not stuck, they are writing. Do not flag them red until 3× this median (360s = 6 minutes).
steps._tasks_note: Four tasks but three p tags — task 1 maps to h1, tasks 2-4 map to the three p tags. The 'something surprising' framing on the third p is pedagogically intentional: it teaches narrative arc (intro → detail → surprise/twist), which maps directly to the K-3 ELA standard for opinion writing structure. DoE reviewers will recognize this.

validation_rules._rule_note: Schema v1.2 rule. Checks ALL three p tags have ≥ 5 chars of content. A student who writes one real paragraph and leaves the others empty passes tag_count but must not pass the challenge. This rule closes that gap. Implementation: querySelectorAll('p'), forEach, check textContent.trim().replace(/\s+/g, '').length >= 5.

byte_lines: Three paragraphs. Your page tells a story now.
Someone could read this and learn something.
You're a writer AND a coder.

lines: Real content. Your words. Your page.
